items.evidence_text:
Client: Thanks. I’d like to feel sure I’m not going to make a mistake I can’t undo—mostly just that my income covers things and I’m not taking on unnecessary risk.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it plain-English and conservative. Let’s start with the big picture: about how much gross income do you have in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All in, my gross income is 84000 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s coming from a few streams, right? I heard rentals and then interest and dividends—can you walk me through those pieces?
Client: Yes. I have rental income of 24100 annually from one place, and another rental income stream of 49450 annually.

Client: Interest & dividends is 10450 annually.
Advisor: Perfect. That helps me see how steady it is month to month. Switching to spending—what do your total monthly expenses usually run, just the regular bills and living costs?
Client: It’s pretty steady—1850 per month.
Advisor: Okay. And any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re paying down—anything like that?
Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client: Thanks. I’d like to feel sure I’m not going to make a mistake I can’t undo—mostly just that my income covers things and I’m not taking on unnecessary risk.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it plain-English and conservative. Let’s start with the big picture: about how much gross income do you have in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All in, my gross income is 84000 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s coming from a few streams, right? I heard rentals and then interest and dividends—can you walk me through those pieces?
Client: Yes. I have rental income of 24100 annually from one place, and another rental income stream of 49450 annually.

Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Great. So no loans outstanding—clean slate there. Now I want to understand what you’ve got set aside. Roughly, what are your investable assets total—accounts you can draw from or manage?
Client: Investable assets total are 315300.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that down so I’m not missing anything. I’m going to go one by one—tell me if I’m off. First, you’ve got a taxable investment account held on a retirement platform—what’s the value there?
Client: That taxable account is 219650.

Client: Yes, it’s 4200.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Separate from investable accounts, you also have your home. What do you use as the current value for your primary residence?
Client: I use 68600 for the primary residence value.
Advisor: Understood. Now, protection—do you have any insurance policies still in force that we should account for?
Client: I do have a disability policy still.

Advisor: Makes sense. And taxes—what bracket do you generally think you’re in these days? If you know it as a percent, that’s perfect.
Client: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Okay. Last housekeeping item: how are we working together—are you looking for an RIA relationship, meaning a registered investment advisor, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just noting that down.

Advisor: Hi David—nice to meet you. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client: Thanks. I’d like to feel sure I’m not going to make a mistake I can’t undo—mostly just that my income covers things and I’m not taking on unnecessary risk.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it plain-English and conservative. Let’s start with the big picture: about how much gross income do you have in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All in, my gross income is 84000 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s coming from a few streams, right? I heard rentals and then interest and dividends—can you walk me through those pieces?

Client: Conservative. I’m conservative—I'd rather not take big swings.
Advisor: Makes sense. And taxes—what bracket do you generally think you’re in these days? If you know it as a percent, that’s perfect.
Client: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Okay. Last housekeeping item: how are we working together—are you looking for an RIA relationship, meaning a registered investment advisor, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).

Client: Yes, that’s correct.
Advisor: Okay, David—thanks for walking through the accounts and the insurance. I want to zoom out for a second and make sure I’ve got the basics of your situation right.
Advisor: First, just to confirm your marital status—are you currently widowed?
Client: Yes, I’m widowed.
Advisor: Got it. And in terms of how you like to invest—when markets get choppy, do you see yourself as more conservative, moderate, or aggressive?

Advisor: Hi David—nice to meet you. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client: Thanks. I’d like to feel sure I’m not going to make a mistake I can’t undo—mostly just that my income covers things and I’m not taking on unnecessary risk.
Advisor: That makes sense. We’ll keep it plain-English and conservative. Let’s start with the big picture: about how much gross income do you have in a typical year, all sources combined?
Client: All in, my gross income is 84000 a year.

Client: All in, my gross income is 84000 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s coming from a few streams, right? I heard rentals and then interest and dividends—can you walk me through those pieces?
Client: Yes. I have rental income of 24100 annually from one place, and another rental income stream of 49450 annually.
Advisor: Okay—two rentals. And then the interest and dividends piece?
Client: Interest & dividends is 10450 annually.

Advisor: And just to separate it cleanly: outside of a mortgage, any non-mortgage debt—credit cards you carry, medical bills on a payment plan—what’s that total?
Client: Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Perfect. When you said earlier “no monthly debt payments,” that lines up—no balances either. Just checking: do you ever float a card month to month, or is it basically paid off as you go?
Client: Basically paid off as I go. I might put things on a card, but I don’t carry a balance.
Advisor: Got it. One small admin thing while we’re here—since you’re the only person in the household, you’re labeled Client 1 on my side. You’re okay with that?

Client: Thanks. I’d like to feel sure I’m not going to make a mistake I can’t undo—mostly just that my income covers things and I’m not taking on unnecessary risk.

Advisor: And just so I’m not missing anything obvious: you’re in FL in the US, one adult in the household, and no dependents. Sound right?
Client: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: Alright, David. Staying zoomed out—can we pin down your work status these days? Are you fully retired, semi-retired, or still doing any work?
Client: I’m retired—fully retired.
Advisor: Got it. And when you think of your “occupation,” it’s basically retired as well—no consulting or anything like that?

Advisor: And there’s a smaller private markets holding on a retirement platform—do you know that value?
Client: Yes, it’s 4200.
